identity.title: Cut Stone and Stone Product Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly template, cut, finish, and fabricate stone products to customer specifications, including countertop, architectural, monument, and commercial stone work sold to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Quarries, stone wholesalers and retailers without fabrication, captive fabrication units, installation-only contractors classified elsewhere, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project and repeat-order revenue from builders, remodelers, designers, institutions, and end customers, commonly quoted per square foot or as a fixed project price covering material, fabrication, options, and sometimes installation.

a.bridge: O*NET measures tasks and work activities for stonemasons rather than the wage-weighted occupation mix of NAICS 327991. I treat the documented physical cutting, positioning, finishing, inspection, and material-handling tasks as minimally exposed, while office administration, quoting, scheduling, CAD assistance, customer communication, and documentation are exposed. This may understate exposure where computer vision and AI-guided equipment improve production planning, but may overstate substitutability where tacit measurement and quality judgment remain essential.
a.rationale: The core work is embodied and material-specific: O*NET lists setting, shaping, cutting, polishing, drilling, and moving stone among central tasks, while administrative work and computer use score much lower. AI can absorb a meaningful minority of wages through quote intake, takeoffs, scheduling, purchasing, document handling, sales support, and design/CAD assistance, but not most shop-floor and field labor within five years.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted OEWS occupation extract for NAICS 327991 combined with task-level time studies from 15-20 countertop, monument, and architectural-stone fabricators would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Stonemasons include construction workers and do not exactly represent manufacturing employees.
No industry-specific time-use study separates already automated CNC work from not-yet-automated tasks.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.

rho.rationale: Much of the exposed opportunity sits in structured office workflows that small operators can implement with packaged quoting, CRM, scheduling, document, and design tools. Realization is constrained by fragmented legacy systems, job-specific exceptions, slab variability, field measurement risk, owner attention, and the need to keep humans accountable for final dimensions and quality.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hour and error-rate data from multi-shop deployments of stone-fabrication ERP, automated takeoff, quoting, and scheduling systems over at least 12 months would materially move this range.
caveats: This is implementation judgment, not an observed five-year adoption rate.
Anthropic usage data overrepresent digital occupations and do not measure stone-shop deployment.

e.rationale: Many LMM firms in this code plausibly provide repeat, outsourced custom fabrication, especially to builders and remodelers, but the industry also includes product-oriented monument, architectural, and other stone manufacturers with episodic end-customer demand or business models outside the repeat-service lens.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of Census Business Register or commercial establishment records, coded by customer type, repeat-order share, fabrication activity, and EBITDA band, would materially improve eligibility.
caveats: The injected 428-firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE and not a directly observed EBITDA-band census.
NAICS establishments do not disclose repeat revenue or customer concentration in public data.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell reports completed sales for small manufacturing businesses across many industries and generally below the stated EBITDA band, not a five-year transfer probability for eligible 327991 firms. The observed 2,303 manufacturing listings sold during 2021-2025 and 2025 median metrics establish that a functioning control-transfer market exists; I translate that liquidity plus broad owner-age evidence into a bounded five-year probability. Selection into brokered listings biases the evidence upward, while unreported private transfers bias it downward.
s5.rationale: Custom manufacturers are demonstrably transferable, and owner aging supports succession supply, but capital intensity, real-estate entanglement, environmental/worker-safety diligence, key-person customer relationships, and a mismatch between marketplace deal size and the target EBITDA band limit the probability for any one eligible firm.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed-deal counts for NAICS 327991 from GF Data, DealStats, BizBuySell, and SBA 7(a) loan records matched to the eligible-firm denominator would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Marketplace transactions are not a census and skew toward smaller businesses.
The Census owner-age table is economy-wide, not stone-manufacturing-specific.
Internal family transfers and asset-only shutdown sales are difficult to distinguish in public data.

q.bridge: Retail and fabricator pricing pages document bundled per-square-foot or project quoting for countertop work, not the discounted five-year benefit-retention share for all 327991 firms. I infer that fixed/bundled quotes initially allow labor savings to remain with the operator, then haircut retention for transparent price comparison, competitive rebidding, contractor negotiation, and renewal repricing. This likely overstates retention for commodity-like work and understates it for differentiated stone, design, and rapid-turnaround work.
q.rationale: A quote often bundles material, templating, fabrication, installation, edges, and cutouts, so customers do not automatically receive an itemized labor-cost reduction. However, per-square-foot comparison is easy, contractor customers rebid, and material is a visible cost component, causing a significant share of benefit to pass through over five years.
q.change_evidence: Cohort gross-margin and realized-price data from fabricators before and after automation, split by builder contracts, retail homeowners, monuments, and architectural projects, would materially move retention.
caveats: Countertop pricing is only one segment of the NAICS code.
Public quotes do not reveal negotiated contractor discounts or renewal behavior.
The range excludes volume loss and implementation difficulty as required.

d5.bridge: USGS reports physical and market trends for dimension stone, while Census reports nominal construction spending; neither directly measures constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current outsourced fabrication basket in NAICS 327991. I use declining dimension-stone sales and soft construction as the near-term anchor, then allow recovery with housing and remodeling. Nominal construction data can overstate real quantity when input prices rise, while imports and engineered-stone substitution can make dimension-stone demand understate total fabrication demand.
d5.rationale: Demand is tied to cyclical residential and nonresidential construction and remodeling. USGS says dimension-stone sales had decreased each year since 2022 and identifies substitutes, while the current Census release shows softer total construction. A flat five-year base balances near-term weakness against replacement/remodeling demand and possible housing recovery.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted quarterly shipment volumes for fabricated stone products, countertop square-meter demand by material, housing completions, and renovation permits through 2031 would materially change the range.
caveats: Dimension stone excludes engineered quartz and other fabricated materials handled by some shops.
Construction spending is nominal and broader than the service basket.
Tariffs, interest rates, and housing turnover add substantial five-year cyclicality.

o.rationale: Most surviving demand still requires an accountable operator to inspect unique slabs, verify site dimensions, plan cuts, manage breakage risk, finish edges, coordinate delivery, and stand behind fit and quality. Software can make selection, design, quoting, and scheduling more self-service, and prefabricated substitutes can remove some custom work, but physical fulfillment cannot become software-only.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level data on adoption of prefabricated countertops, direct-to-consumer digital ordering, robotic fabrication, rework rates, and the share of orders completed without local templating or accountable installation coordination would materially move this estimate.
caveats: The estimate distinguishes operator requirement from labor intensity; automation may reduce headcount without eliminating the operator.
Substitution toward laminate, porcelain, resin-agglomerated stone, and standardized modules could reduce custom operator demand faster than assumed.

narrative.executive_view: Cut-stone fabrication combines a highly physical production core with a smaller but meaningful layer of quotable, schedulable, document-heavy work. AI is more likely to tighten the office and planning system around the shop than to replace cutting, finishing, handling, and final quality accountability.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term use cases are quote intake, drawing and takeoff assistance, option configuration, scheduling, purchasing, customer updates, collections, sales follow-up, and management reporting. Human checks remain essential because unique materials, site measurements, breakage, seams, and finish quality create costly exceptions.
narrative.value_capture: Bundled fixed or per-square-foot pricing can retain early productivity gains, but transparent comparison among local fabricators and frequent project rebidding should pass a material share to customers over time.
narrative.firm_availability: A brokered market exists for small manufacturers, but eligibility and transferability are uncertain at the target size. Equipment, property, owner relationships, and environmental and safety diligence can complicate control transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: The current basket remains tied to construction and remodeling. Physical demand may be roughly flat over five years, with downside from housing weakness and substitute materials and upside from renewed housing turnover and renovation.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest evidence gaps are the true occupation/time mix, the share of firms with repeat B2B revenue, and realized post-automation margins. Material substitution, silica and safety compliance, tariff exposure, cyclicality, and rework liability could overwhelm office productivity gains.
narrative.principal_driver: Bundled custom-project pricing creates room to retain gains from automating fragmented quoting and coordination work.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and exception-heavy, while demand is cyclical and exposed to cheaper substitute surfaces.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Stonemasons (47-2022.00)
sources.supports: Lists core physical tasks including setting, cutting, shaping, polishing, drilling, and moving stone; also reports lower-scored administrative and computer activities used to bound AI-exposed work.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Cadences
sources.supports: Reports that physical occupation categories such as construction and material moving are underrepresented in Claude use and cautions that its user survey is not workforce-representative.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index: Uneven geographic and enterprise AI adoption
sources.supports: Reports that first-party API use is automation-dominant and especially oriented toward coding and office/administrative tasks, supporting implementability for digital workflows rather than physical fabrication.

sources.title: Manufacturing Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Reports 2,303 sold manufacturing listings analyzed for 2021-2025, median revenue of $1,127,990, median owner earnings of $268,321, and median 2025 sale price of $650,000.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey: Characteristics of Business Owners, 2022 Tables
sources.supports: Provides economy-wide employer-business owner characteristics including the Age of Owner table, used only as a broad succession anchor.

sources.title: Countertop Granite Astoria pricing page
sources.supports: Shows a per-square-foot price including material, fabrication, installation, a template visit, basic edges, and one sink cutout, with final job-site verification.

sources.title: How Do Fabricators Price Countertops?
sources.supports: Describes prevailing per-square-foot quotes that bundle material, fabrication, standard edge work, cutouts, templating, installation, and sealing.

sources.title: USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries 2026: Stone (Dimension)
sources.supports: States that US dimension-stone sales were estimated to have decreased each year since 2022, discusses housing and construction conditions, and lists material substitutes.

sources.title: US Census Bureau: Monthly Construction Spending, May 2026
sources.supports: Reports total May 2026 construction spending 1.5% below May 2025 and first-five-month spending 2.7% below the comparable 2025 period, with residential and nonresidential detail.
